title: Guanabara Bay

infobox.value: * [Acari](/wiki/Acari_River_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Acari River (Rio de Janeiro)") * [Carioca](/wiki/Carioca_River "Carioca River") * [Iguaçu](/wiki/Igua%C3%A7u_River_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Iguaçu River (Rio de Janeiro)") * [Macacu](/wiki/Macacu_River "Macacu River") * [Suruí](/wiki/Suru%C3%AD_River "Suruí River") * and others

[Ilha do Governador](/wiki/Ilha_do_Governador "Ilha do Governador"), [Ilha de Paquetá](/wiki/Ilha_de_Paquet%C3%A1 "Ilha de Paquetá"), [Freguesia](/wiki/Freguesia_(Ilha_do_Governador),_Rio_de_Janeiro "Freguesia (Ilha do Governador), Rio de Janeiro")

[Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Niterói](/wiki/Niter%C3%B3i "Niterói"), [Duque de Caxias](/wiki/Duque_de_Caxias,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Duque de Caxias, Rio de Janeiro"), [São Gonçalo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Gon%C3%A7alo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "São Gonçalo, Rio de Janeiro")

([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *baía de Guanabara*, *baía da Guanabara*,[[1]](#cite_note-1) IPA: [[baˈi.ɐ da ɡwɐnaˈbaɾɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is an oceanic [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay") in [Southeast Brazil](/wiki/Southeast_Brazil "Southeast Brazil") in the state of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"). On its western [shore](/wiki/Shore "Shore") lie the cities of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(city) "Rio de Janeiro (city)") and [Duque de Caxias](/wiki/Duque_de_Caxias,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Duque de Caxias, Rio de Janeiro"), and on its eastern shore are the cities of [Niterói](/wiki/Niter%C3%B3i "Niterói") and [São Gonçalo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Gon%C3%A7alo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "São Gonçalo, Rio de Janeiro"). Four other municipalities surround the bay's shores. Guanabara Bay is the second largest bay in area in Brazil (after the [All Saints' Bay](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Bay "All Saints' Bay")), at 412 square kilometres (159 sq mi), with a perimeter of 143 kilometres (89 mi).

**Guanabara Bay** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *baía de Guanabara*, *baía da Guanabara*,[[1]](#cite_note-1) IPA: [[baˈi.ɐ da ɡwɐnaˈbaɾɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is an oceanic [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay") in [Southeast Brazil](/wiki/Southeast_Brazil "Southeast Brazil") in the state of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"). On its western [shore](/wiki/Shore "Shore") lie the cities of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(city) "Rio de Janeiro (city)") and [Duque de Caxias](/wiki/Duque_de_Caxias,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Duque de Caxias, Rio de Janeiro"), and on its eastern shore are the cities of [Niterói](/wiki/Niter%C3%B3i "Niterói") and [São Gonçalo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Gon%C3%A7alo,_Rio_de_Janeiro "São Gonçalo, Rio de Janeiro"). Four other municipalities surround the bay's shores. Guanabara Bay is the second largest bay in area in Brazil (after the [All Saints' Bay](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Bay "All Saints' Bay")), at 412 square kilometres (159 sq mi), with a perimeter of 143 kilometres (89 mi).

Guanabara Bay is 31 kilometres (19 mi) long and 28 kilometres (17 mi) wide at its maximum. Its 1.5 kilometres (0.93 mi) wide mouth is flanked at the eastern tip by the Pico do Papagaio (Parrot's Peak) and the western tip by [Pão de Açúcar](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Sugarloaf Mountain, Rio de Janeiro") (Sugar Loaf).

The name Guanabara comes from the [Tupi language](/wiki/Tupi_language "Tupi language"), *goanã-pará*, from *gwa* "bay", plus *nã* "similar to" and *ba'ra* "sea".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other glosses include *hidden water*,[[2]](#cite_note-Franco-2) *lagoon of the sea*,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and *bosom of the sea*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guanabara_Bay&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/70/Guanabara_Bay_1900.jpg/250px-Guanabara_Bay_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guanabara_Bay_1900.jpg)

View of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") from Guanabara Bay (early 20th century picture).

Guanabara Bay was first encountered by Europeans on January 1, 1502, when Portuguese explorers [Gaspar de Lemos](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Lemos "Gaspar de Lemos") and [Gonçalo Coelho](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Coelho "Gonçalo Coelho")[[5]](#cite_note-5) arrived on its shores. According to some historians,[[6]](#cite_note-6) the name given by the exploration team to the bay was originally *Ria de Janeiro* ("January's [Ria](/wiki/Ria "Ria")"). Then confusion took place between the words *ria* and *rio* ("river"). As a result, the name of the bay was soon fixed as *Rio de Janeiro*.
Later, the city was named after the bay. [Natives](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") of the [Tamoio](/wiki/Tamoio "Tamoio") and [Tupiniquim](/wiki/Tupiniquim "Tupiniquim") tribes inhabited the shores of the bay.

After the initial arrival of the Portuguese, no significant European settlements were established until [French colonists](/wiki/French_invasions_in_Brazil "French invasions in Brazil") and soldiers, under the [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") Admiral [Nicolas Durand de Villegaignon](/wiki/Nicolas_Durand_de_Villegaignon "Nicolas Durand de Villegaignon") invaded the region in 1555 to establish the [France Antarctique](/wiki/France_Antarctique "France Antarctique"). They stayed briefly on Lajes Island, then moved to Serigipe Island, near the shore, where they built [Fort Coligny](/wiki/Fort_Coligny "Fort Coligny"). After they were expelled by Portuguese military expeditions in 1563, the colonial government built fortifications in several points of Guanabara Bay, rendering it almost impregnable against a naval attack from the sea. They were the Santa Cruz, São João, Lajes and Villegaignon forts, forming a fearsome crossfire rectangle of big [naval guns](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"). Other islands were adapted by the [Navy](/wiki/Navy "Navy") to host naval storehouses, [hospitals](/wiki/Hospitals "Hospitals"), [drydocks](/wiki/Drydock "Drydock"), [oil reservoirs](/wiki/Oil_reservoir "Oil reservoir") and the [National Naval Academy](/w/index.php?title=Naval_Academy_(Brazil)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Naval Academy (Brazil) (page does not exist)").

Underwater exploration in the bay was disallowed by the Brazilian government in 1985 amid a dispute with American writer and treasure hunter Robert Marx, who claimed to have found evidence of a Roman shipwreck.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Carl Feagans tells of 16 amphorae made for antiques-lover Americo Santarelli in 1960 or 1961, all 16 of which deliberately sunk in the bay for them to acquire the look of ancient artefacts covered in barnacles and corals; 4 of the 16 original amphorae were subsequently recovered, leaving 12 scattered about the bay, where two were found by lobster divers in 1974.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guanabara_Bay&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_Cristo.jpg/330px-Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_Cristo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_Cristo.jpg)

Guanabara Bay seen from [Christ the Redeemer](/wiki/Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue) "Christ the Redeemer (statue)").

There are more than 130 islands dotting the bay, including:

* [Lajes](/w/index.php?title=Lajes_(island)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lajes (island) (page does not exist)")
* [Ilha do Governador](/wiki/Governador_Island "Governador Island") – site of Rio de Janeiro's [Galeão - Antônio Carlos Jobim International Airport](/wiki/Gale%C3%A3o_-_Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim_International_Airport "Galeão - Antônio Carlos Jobim International Airport")
* [Ilha de Paquetá](/wiki/Paquet%C3%A1_Island "Paquetá Island")
* [Ilha das Cobras](/wiki/Ilha_das_Cobras "Ilha das Cobras")
* [Flores](/w/index.php?title=Flores_(island)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Flores (island) (page does not exist)")
* [Ilha Fiscal](/wiki/Ilha_Fiscal "Ilha Fiscal")
* [Ilha da Boa Viagem](/w/index.php?title=Ilha_da_Boa_Viagem&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ilha da Boa Viagem (page does not exist)")
* [Villegagnon](/wiki/Villegagnon_Island "Villegagnon Island")
* [Fundão](/w/index.php?title=Fund%C3%A3o_(island)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fundão (island) (page does not exist)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_2020.jpg/330px-Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ba%C3%ADa_de_Guanabara_vista_do_P%C3%A3o_de_A%C3%A7%C3%BAcar_2020.jpg)

Guanabara Bay seen from [Sugarloaf Mountain](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain "Sugarloaf Mountain").

The bay is crossed by the [Rio-Niterói Bridge](/wiki/Rio-Niter%C3%B3i_Bridge "Rio-Niterói Bridge") (13.29 kilometres (8.26 mi) long and with a central span 72 metres (236 ft) high) and there is heavy boat and ship traffic, including regular [ferryboat](/wiki/Ferryboat "Ferryboat") lines. The Port of Rio de Janeiro, as well as the city's two airports, [Galeão - Antônio Carlos Jobim International Airport](/wiki/Gale%C3%A3o_-_Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Jobim_International_Airport "Galeão - Antônio Carlos Jobim International Airport") (on Governador Island) and [Santos Dumont Airport](/wiki/Santos_Dumont_Airport "Santos Dumont Airport") (on [reclaimed land](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") next to downtown Rio), are on its shores. The [Federal University of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Federal_University_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Federal University of Rio de Janeiro") main campus is on the artificial Fundão Island. A maze of smaller bridges interconnect the two largest islands, Fundão and Governador, to the mainland.

There is an [Environmental Protection Area](/wiki/Environmental_Protection_Area_(Brazil) "Environmental Protection Area (Brazil)") (APA), which is mostly in the municipality of Guapimirim and given the name of [Guapimirim APA](/wiki/Guapimirim_Environmental_Protection_Area "Guapimirim Environmental Protection Area").

## Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guanabara_Bay&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Environment")]

Guanabara Bay's once rich and diversified [ecosystem](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") has suffered extensive damage in recent decades, particularly along its [mangrove](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") areas.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The bay has been heavily impacted by [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), and [pollution](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution") of its waters with [sewage](/wiki/Sewage "Sewage"), [garbage](/wiki/Waste "Waste"), and [oil spills](/wiki/Oil_spills "Oil spills"). As of 2014, more than 70% of the sewage from 12 million inhabitants of Rio de Janeiro now flows into the bay untreated.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

There have been three major [oil spills in Guanabara Bay](/wiki/Guanabara_Bay_oil_spill_(disambiguation) "Guanabara Bay oil spill (disambiguation)"). The most recent was in 2000 when a leaking [Petrobras](/wiki/Petrobras "Petrobras") underwater pipeline released 1,300,000 litres (340,000 US gal) of oil into the bay, destroying large swaths of the [mangrove](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") ecosystem. Recovery measures are currently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] being attempted, but more than a decade after the incident, the mangrove areas have not returned to life.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

One of the world's largest [landfills](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill") is at [Jardim Gramacho](/wiki/Jardim_Gramacho "Jardim Gramacho") adjacent to Guanabara Bay. It was closed in 2012 after 34 years of operation. The landfill attracted attention from environmentalists and it supported 1,700 people scavenging for recyclable materials.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Guanabara_Bay_pan.jpg/500px-Guanabara_Bay_pan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guanabara_Bay_pan.jpg)

View of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") from Guanabara Bay

In June 2014, Dutch windsurfer and former Olympic and world champion [Dorian van Rijsselberghe](/wiki/Dorian_van_Rijsselberghe "Dorian van Rijsselberghe") made an urgent appeal to government and industry in the Netherlands to collaborate in cleaning up the bay, together with the [Plastic Soup Foundation](/wiki/Plastic_Soup_Foundation "Plastic Soup Foundation").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Dutch government picked up the message and formulated a Clean Urban Delta Initiative Rio de Janeiro together with a consortium of Dutch industry, knowledge institutes and NGOs which will be presented to the Brazilian authorities in the State of Rio de Janeiro.

As part of the preparations for the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") in Rio, the government was supposed to improve the conditions, but progress has been slow. There have been concerns that the efforts may only be short-term and abandoned following the Games, as there would be little political incentive to continue with them.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The marine ecosystem of Guanabara Bay was severely damaged;[[15]](#cite_note-15) the bay was once a whaling ground,[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) and today, whales are no longer or rarely seen while [Bryde's whales](/wiki/Bryde%27s_whale "Bryde's whale") can be seen around the bay entrance.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The bay is also home to a population of [botos](/wiki/Boto "Boto")[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) and this population faces severe risks of [population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline").[[25]](#cite_note-25)
